records.ety: [OE. deinie, dainte, deintie, deyntee, OF. deinti delicacy, orig., dignity, honor, fr. L. dignitas, fr. dignus worthy. See Deign, and cf. Dignity.]
usage: These words are here compared as denoting articles of food. The term delicacy as applied to a nice article of any kind, and hence to articles of food which are particularly attractive. Dainty is stronger, and denotes some exquisite article of cookery. A hotel may be provided with all the delicacies of the season, and its table richly covered with dainties.

defs: Value; estimation; the gratification or pleasure taken in anything.
quotes: I ne told no deyntee of her love.

defs: That which is delicious or delicate; a delicacy.
quotes: That precious nectar may the taste renew Of Eden's dainties, by our parents lost.
These delicacies I mean of taste, sight, smell, herbs, fruits, and flowers, Walks and the melody of birds.
[A table] furnished plenteously with bread, And dainties, remnants of the last regale.
Full many a deynt horse had he in stable.
notes: ☞ Hence the proverb “dainty maketh dearth,” i. e., rarity makes a thing dear or precious.

quotes: Dainty bits Make rich the ribs.

defs: Nice; delicate; elegant, in form, manner, or breeding; well-formed; neat; tender.
quotes: Those dainty limbs which nature lent For gentle usage and soft delicacy.
I would be the girdle. About her dainty, dainty waist.

defs: Requiring dainties. Hence: Overnice; hard to please; fastidious; squeamish; scrupulous; ceremonious.
quotes: Thew were a fine and dainty people.
And let us not be dainty of leave-taking, But shift away.
Ah ha, my mistresses! which of you all Will now deny to dance? She that makes dainty, She, I'll swear, hath corns.
